questions.q: In Hindu tradition, what kind of object is the Parijata?
questions.explanation: The Parijata is celebrated in the Puranas as the wish-bestowing celestial flowering tree.

questions.q: From which great event did the Parijata tree arise?
options: The churning of the ocean of milk
questions.explanation: The Parijata was one of the treasures that emerged from the Samudra Manthana, the churning of the ocean of milk.

questions.q: After it arose from the ocean, in whose heaven was the Parijata tree planted?
questions.explanation: The Parijata was planted in the heaven of Indra, king of the devas, where it perfumed the celestial realm.

questions.q: Which avatar of Vishnu is famed for bringing the Parijata tree from Indra's garden to his own city?
questions.explanation: Krishna brought the Parijata from Indra's heaven down to his city, as recounted in the Harivamsha and Bhagavata Purana.

questions.q: To which of Krishna's queens did he bring the Parijata tree to please her?
questions.explanation: Krishna brought the Parijata to please his queen Satyabhama, according to the Harivamsha and Bhagavata Purana.

questions.q: To which of Krishna's cities was the Parijata tree carried from Indra's heaven?
questions.explanation: Krishna carried the Parijata to Dvaraka, his island capital, to please Satyabhama.

questions.q: The Parijata is best classified as which type of sacred item in Hindu lore?
questions.explanation: The Parijata is a divine treasure of the churning, revered as the wish-bestowing celestial tree.

questions.q: In the churning that produced the Parijata, which two opposing groups pulled the churning rope?
questions.explanation: The devas and asuras together churned the ocean of milk in the Samudra Manthana, from which the Parijata arose.

questions.q: Which quality is traditionally ascribed to the blossoms of the Parijata tree?
options: They turn to stone at dawn
They are poisonous to the gods
They bloom only once in an age
They never fade and perfume the worlds
questions.explanation: The Parijata's blossoms are said never to fade, perfuming the worlds with their fragrance.

questions.q: The texts that recount Krishna bringing the Parijata to Dvaraka belong chiefly to which bodies of literature?
questions.explanation: The Parijata episode is narrated in the Harivamsha and in Puranas such as the Bhagavata and Vishnu Puranas.

questions.q: The bringing of the Parijata to Dvaraka is said to have sparked a celebrated conflict between Krishna and which deity?
questions.explanation: Removing the Parijata from heaven sparked a celebrated contest between Krishna and Indra, in whose garden it grew.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, which mountain served as the churning rod during the Samudra Manthana that yielded the Parijata?
questions.explanation: Mount Mandara was used as the churning rod in the Samudra Manthana that produced the Parijata.

questions.q: Which serpent was used as the churning rope during the Samudra Manthana that produced the Parijata?
questions.explanation: The serpent Vasuki was wound about Mount Mandara to serve as the churning rope.

questions.q: The Parijata is counted among the treasures of the ocean churning, the same event that also produced which nectar of immortality?
questions.explanation: The Samudra Manthana produced both the Parijata tree and Amrita, the nectar of immortality.

questions.q: In the celestial pleasure garden of Indra's heaven, where the Parijata is said to grow, what is that famed garden called?
questions.explanation: The Parijata grows in Nandana, the pleasure garden of Indra's heaven described in the Puranas.

questions.q: In the Samudra Manthana, the avatar of Vishnu who supported the sinking Mount Mandara on his back was which form?
questions.explanation: Vishnu took the form of Kurma, the tortoise, to bear Mount Mandara upon his back during the churning.

questions.q: The Parijata is one of the auspicious treasures of the churning; which goddess of fortune also arose from the same waters?
questions.explanation: Lakshmi, the goddess of fortune and consort of Vishnu, emerged from the churning that also yielded the Parijata.

questions.q: In the Harivamsha, after Krishna obtained the Parijata, in whose courtyard or garden was the tree planted at Dvaraka?
questions.explanation: The tree was planted in the garden of Satyabhama, for whose sake Krishna had brought the Parijata to Dvaraka.

questions.q: The Parijata is described as a tree that grants wishes; such a wish-fulfilling celestial tree is known by which general term?
questions.explanation: The Parijata is reckoned among the kalpavriksha, the wish-fulfilling celestial trees of the gods.

questions.q: Which queen of Krishna is traditionally contrasted with Satyabhama in the Parijata story for receiving only a single blossom rather than the whole tree?
questions.explanation: In the tale, Krishna gives a Parijata blossom to Rukmini while the tree itself is planted for Satyabhama, a contrast central to the story.

questions.q: The Samudra Manthana that yielded the Parijata is most fully narrated in which body of texts and the epics?
questions.explanation: The churning of the ocean is narrated in the Puranas and in the Mahabharata.

questions.q: The Parijata emerged from the ocean of milk, which in Sanskrit cosmology is named what?
questions.explanation: The ocean of milk that was churned is called the Kshira Sagara in Sanskrit.

questions.q: In the Harivamsha account, who accompanies Krishna to Indra's heaven and aids him in taking the Parijata?
questions.explanation: In the Harivamsha, Satyabhama rides with Krishna on Garuda to Indra's heaven when the Parijata is taken.

questions.q: On which mount does Krishna fly to Indra's heaven to obtain the Parijata in the Puranic narrative?
questions.explanation: Krishna rides his eagle-mount Garuda to Indra's heaven to bring back the Parijata.

questions.q: According to the tradition surrounding the Parijata episode, who was the consort of Indra that wished to keep the tree in heaven?
questions.explanation: Shachi, also called Indrani, the consort of Indra, is associated with the heavenly Parijata that Krishna removed.

questions.q: In the Bhagavata Purana, after Krishna defeats Indra in the contest over the Parijata, what is said of their relationship at the episode's close?
options: The tree is destroyed in the strife
questions.explanation: The episode closes with reconciliation: Indra yields the Parijata, and harmony is restored between him and Krishna.

questions.q: During the same Samudra Manthana that produced the Parijata, the dreadful poison that first arose was consumed by which deity?
questions.explanation: Shiva drank the Halahala poison that emerged before the treasures, including the Parijata, appeared.

questions.q: The Parijata is grouped among the ratnas (treasures) of the churning; which celestial wish-granting cow is likewise counted among those treasures?
questions.explanation: Kamadhenu, the wish-fulfilling cow, is among the celebrated treasures (ratnas) that arose from the churning alongside the Parijata.

questions.q: In the Harivamsha, what is said to become of the Parijata tree after Krishna's earthly sojourn concludes?
options: It is given to the Pandavas
It sinks back into the ocean
It is returned to Indra's heaven
questions.explanation: The Harivamsha relates that the Parijata is restored to Indra's heaven after the close of Krishna's earthly life.

questions.q: The Parijata belongs to the cluster of treasures from the churning; which of the following is the physician-god who rose from the same ocean, rather than a tree or plant?
questions.explanation: Dhanvantari, the physician of the gods bearing the pot of Amrita, rose from the churned ocean, distinct from the Parijata tree.
